title: Energy cycles in reactions
summary: Some enthalpy changes cannot be measured on a balance and a thermometer — the reaction is too slow, too messy, or simply will not happen on its own. Hess's law rescues these cases: because enthalpy is a state function, the enthalpy change of a reaction depends only on the start and finish, not the path taken. This lesson builds the whole R1.2 toolkit around that idea — stating and justifying Hess's law, constructing enthalpy cycles, defining standard enthalpies of formation and combustion, and calculating an unknown $\Delta H_{reaction}$ from $\Delta H_f$ data (Σproducts − Σreactants) and from combustion data. It closes with the HL extension — Born–Haber cycles, lattice enthalpy and enthalpy of solution — and every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks.
learningObjectives: State Hess's law and justify it from the fact that enthalpy is a state function, so $\Delta H$ is independent of the path taken.
Construct enthalpy cycles from standard enthalpies of formation and of combustion, and read the correct relationship off the arrows.

Define standard enthalpy of formation ($\Delta H_f^\ominus$) and standard enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c^\ominus$), and know that $\Delta H_f^\ominus$ of an element in its standard state is zero.
Calculate $\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus$ from formation data using $\sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{reactants})$, and from combustion data using a cycle.
(HL) Construct Born–Haber cycles, and use them with Hess's law to find lattice enthalpy and enthalpy of solution.
Present working so that method marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) protect the final answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL; Born–Haber cycles and lattice enthalpy are HL.

simpleExplanation.title: The Chemical Detour
simpleExplanation.summary: Hess's law says the total energy change of a reaction is the same no matter which route you take from reactants to products. So an enthalpy change you cannot measure directly can be found by adding up the steps of an easier, indirect route that starts and ends at the same place.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of the altitude difference between the base and the summit of a mountain. Measuring it as a single vertical drop is awkward, but you can hike any winding trail to the top and add up the small ups and downs along the way — the net change in height is identical for every route, because height depends only on where you start and finish. Enthalpy behaves exactly like altitude: it is a state function, so the enthalpy change depends only on the initial and final states, not the path.
steps: Write the target reaction whose enthalpy change $\Delta H_{reaction}$ you want — this is the 'direct route'.
Choose an 'indirect route' that links the same reactants and products through a common set of species, using enthalpy values you already know (formation or combustion).
Sketch the cycle and mark every arrow with its known $\Delta H$; going with an arrow adds it, going against an arrow subtracts it (reverse the sign).
Apply Hess's law: the direct route equals the sum of the indirect route, and solve for the unknown $\Delta H_{reaction}$.

flashcards.back: The total enthalpy change for a reaction is independent of the route taken, provided the initial and final conditions (and states) are the same. It follows directly from conservation of energy.
A property whose value depends only on the current state of the system, not on how that state was reached. Enthalpy $H$ is a state function — that is why $\Delta H$ is path-independent.
The enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states, under standard conditions (100 kPa, a stated temperature, usually 298 K).
The enthalpy change when one mole of a substance undergoes complete combustion in excess oxygen under standard conditions. Always negative (combustion is exothermic).
Zero, by definition. Forming an element from itself in its standard state (e.g. O₂(g), Na(s), C(s, graphite)) involves no change, so there is nothing to release or absorb.

$\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{reactants})$. Products minus reactants — because the elements sit at the BOTTOM of a formation cycle.

$\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = \sum \Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{reactants}) - \sum \Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{products})$. Reactants minus products — the reverse order — because the combustion products sit at the BOTTOM of a combustion cycle.

If you traverse a known reaction backwards in a cycle, invert the sign of its $\Delta H$. An exothermic forward step ($\Delta H < 0$) becomes endothermic in reverse ($\Delta H > 0$) with the same magnitude.

Enthalpy values are per mole of substance, so multiply each by its stoichiometric coefficient in the balanced equation before summing (e.g. 3 mol H₂ contributes $3 \times \Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{H}_2)$).

It gives enthalpy changes for reactions that cannot be measured directly — too slow (graphite → diamond), too dangerous, or accompanied by side reactions — by routing through species with known enthalpies.
The constituent elements sit at the bottom; arrows point UP from the elements to the reactants and to the products.
The combustion products (CO₂(g) and H₂O(l)) sit at the bottom; arrows point DOWN from the reactants and from the products to those combustion products.

The enthalpy change when one mole of a solid ionic compound is formed from its gaseous ions (an exothermic, lattice-formation convention). It cannot be measured directly, so it is found from a Born–Haber cycle.

An enthalpy cycle that links the formation of an ionic solid to a sequence of steps — atomisation, ionisation energies, electron affinities and lattice enthalpy — so an unmeasurable step can be found by Hess's law.
The enthalpy change when one mole of a solute dissolves in enough solvent to give an infinitely dilute solution. Found from lattice enthalpy and the hydration enthalpies of the ions via a cycle.

quickCheck.prompt: A cycle contains the known step A → B with $\Delta H = -150$ kJ, but your indirect route needs to go B → A. What value do you use for that step?
Using standard enthalpies of FORMATION, the correct expression for $\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus$ is:
What is the standard enthalpy of formation of oxygen gas, O₂(g), at 298 K?
options: It cannot be determined without data

quickCheck.prompt: For 2Al(s) + Fe₂O₃(s) → Al₂O₃(s) + 2Fe(s), which terms are needed in Σ$\Delta H_f$(reactants)?
options: $\Delta H_f$(Fe₂O₃) only — the elements Al and Fe are zero
quickCheck.answer: $\Delta H_f$(Fe₂O₃) only — the elements Al and Fe are zero

quickCheck.prompt: A combustion cycle gives 3 mol of H₂ combusting, with $\Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{H}_2) = -286$ kJ mol⁻¹. The contribution of the hydrogen to that side of the cycle is:

faq.q: Do I have to draw the cycle, or can I just use the formula?
faq.a: For a clean set of formation or combustion data you can earn full marks with the formula alone. But a quick sketch of the cycle is the safest habit: it shows you which formula applies (products − reactants for formation, reactants − products for combustion), and it makes sign errors obvious because you simply follow the arrows. When a question mixes data types, the cycle is not optional — it is the only reliable route.

faq.q: Why is it 'products − reactants' for formation but 'reactants − products' for combustion?
faq.a: It comes straight from where the common species sit in each cycle. In a formation cycle the elements are at the BOTTOM, so the indirect route goes up from reactants and down to products — algebraically that gives Σproducts − Σreactants. In a combustion cycle the combustion products are at the bottom, so the route goes down from reactants and up to products, reversing the order to Σreactants − Σproducts. If you ever forget, redraw the cycle and read the arrows.

faq.q: What if a question gives a mixture of formation and combustion data?
faq.a: Neither standard formula applies directly. Build a bespoke cycle: place your target reactants and products, then use each piece of given data as an arrow linking them to a common intermediate (elements, or combustion products). Add the steps along a complete path from reactants to products, reversing the sign of any step you travel backwards. This is exactly the Hess's-law reasoning the formulae are shortcuts for.

faq.q: (HL) Why can't lattice enthalpy just be measured directly?
faq.a: You cannot take a beaker of isolated gaseous Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions and watch them snap into a crystal — that state does not exist to hand. So lattice enthalpy is found indirectly with a Born–Haber cycle: every other step (atomisation, ionisation energy, electron affinity, enthalpy of formation) can be measured, and Hess's law lets you solve for the one unknown. It is the same detour logic as the rest of R1.2, applied to ionic solids.

sections.content: In thermochemistry we constantly want the enthalpy change of a particular reaction, but not every reaction cooperates with a calorimeter. Some are too slow to measure, like graphite turning into diamond; some run alongside messy side reactions; some cannot be made to happen in isolation at all. Energy cycles and Hess's law are the way around this: because enthalpy is a state function, the enthalpy change of a reaction depends only on the initial and final states, so we can reach an unmeasurable value along an indirect route built from enthalpies we already know. This lesson is calculation-heavy, and every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks — because in R1.2 how you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
Hess's law and why it works

Hess's law states that the total enthalpy change during a reaction is the same regardless of the route by which the reaction is carried out. It is not a separate rule of nature but a direct consequence of the conservation of energy: enthalpy is a state function, so its value depends only on the current state of the system (temperature, pressure, composition), not on the path taken to get there. The enthalpy difference between reactants and products is therefore fixed. If it were not — if one route released more energy than another — you could loop around the cycle and create energy from nothing.

items: Enthalpy $H$ is a state function; $\Delta H$ depends only on the initial and final states.
Hess's law: the enthalpy change of a reaction is independent of the pathway taken between fixed start and end points.
It lets us calculate enthalpy changes that cannot be measured directly, by routing through species with known enthalpies.
States must match at the start and end (e.g. H₂O(l) is not H₂O(g)) or the enthalpy values will not be consistent.

sections.content: Constructing enthalpy cycles

An enthalpy cycle is the picture of Hess's law. The target reaction with the unknown $\Delta H_{reaction}$ is the 'direct route' along the top; the 'indirect route' links the same reactants and products through a common set of species. The two workhorse cycles use standard enthalpies of formation ($\Delta H_f^\ominus$), where the common species are the constituent elements, and standard enthalpies of combustion ($\Delta H_c^\ominus$), where the common species are the products of complete combustion. Once the cycle is drawn, the rule is mechanical: travelling WITH an arrow adds its $\Delta H$; travelling AGAINST an arrow subtracts it (reverse the sign).

items: **Formation cycles:** the constituent elements in their standard states sit at the bottom; arrows point UP to reactants and products.
**Combustion cycles:** the complete-combustion products (CO₂(g) and H₂O(l)) sit at the bottom; arrows point DOWN from reactants and products.
Mark every arrow with its known $\Delta H$, remembering to multiply by the stoichiometric coefficient of each substance.
Read the relationship by following a complete path from reactants to products: with-arrow adds, against-arrow subtracts.

sections.content: Standard enthalpies of formation and combustion

The standard enthalpy of formation, $\Delta H_f^\ominus$, is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound forms from its constituent elements in their standard states under standard conditions (100 kPa, usually 298 K). A crucial consequence: the $\Delta H_f^\ominus$ of any element in its standard state — O₂(g), Na(s), C(s, graphite) — is exactly zero, because forming an element from itself is no change at all. The standard enthalpy of combustion, $\Delta H_c^\ominus$, is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance burns completely in excess oxygen under standard conditions; it is always negative because combustion is exothermic. These two quantities anchor the two standard cycles.
Worked example 1 — a Hess-cycle calculation

sections.question:
Carbon can be oxidised in two steps. Using the cycle data below, calculate $\Delta H$ for the direct combustion of carbon to carbon dioxide, C(s) + O₂(g) → CO₂(g).

Step 1: C(s) + ½O₂(g) → CO(g), $\Delta H_1 = -111$ kJ mol⁻¹
Step 2: CO(g) + ½O₂(g) → CO₂(g), $\Delta H_2 = -283$ kJ mol⁻¹ [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — see the cycle.** The direct route C(s) + O₂(g) → CO₂(g) can be reached indirectly by going C → CO (Step 1) and then CO → CO₂ (Step 2). Both indirect steps run in the same forward direction as the target, so neither sign is reversed.  [M1: correct route — target = Step 1 + Step 2]

**Step 2 — apply Hess's law along the indirect route.**
$\Delta H_{reaction} = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2$
$\Delta H_{reaction} = (-111) + (-283)$  [M1: correct substitution]

**Step 3 — evaluate.**
$\Delta H_{reaction} = -394$ kJ mol⁻¹.  [A1: answer with negative sign and units]

*Both steps are exothermic and travelled forwards, so they simply add. If the target had instead been CO₂ → C + O₂, every sign would flip to give $+394$ kJ mol⁻¹.*

sections.content: Calculations using enthalpies of formation

In a formation cycle the elements sit at the bottom. The indirect route 'decomposes' the reactants into their elements (the reverse of formation, so those signs flip) and then 'forms' the products from those same elements. Reversing the reactant side is exactly what turns the relationship into products minus reactants.
\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{reactants})
Worked example 2 — a ΣΔH_f calculation

sections.question:
Calculate the standard enthalpy change for the hydrogenation of propene to propane: C₃H₆(g) + H₂(g) → C₃H₈(g).

$\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{C}_3\text{H}_6, g) = +20.4$ kJ mol⁻¹
$\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8, g) = -104.7$ kJ mol⁻¹ [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — choose the formation formula and list the species.**
Products: C₃H₈(g). Reactants: C₃H₆(g) and H₂(g). H₂(g) is an element in its standard state, so $\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{H}_2, g) = 0$.  [M1: correct method, Σproducts − Σreactants]

**Step 2 — substitute.**
$\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = [\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8)] - [\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{C}_3\text{H}_6) + \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{H}_2)]$
$\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = [-104.7] - [(+20.4) + 0]$  [M1: correct substitution, including 0 for H₂]

**Step 3 — evaluate.**
$\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = -104.7 - 20.4 = -125.1$ kJ mol⁻¹.  [A1: answer with sign and units]

*Hydrogenation is exothermic, consistent with the negative value. The zero for H₂ is doing real work here — leaving it out is one of the most common errors.*

sections.content: Calculations using enthalpies of combustion

In a combustion cycle the common species are the combustion products at the bottom. The indirect route combusts the reactants (downwards) and then reverses the combustion of the products to climb back up to them. Reversing the product side is what flips the relationship into reactants minus products — the mirror image of the formation formula.
\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = \sum \Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{reactants}) - \sum \Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{products})

The single most common mix-up is the order of subtraction. Formation is 'products − reactants'; combustion is 'reactants − products'. If you are ever unsure, sketch the cycle and follow the arrows: going with an arrow is positive, against an arrow is negative. The arrows never lie, and they will always hand you the correct order.
Worked example 3 — a combustion-data cycle

sections.question:
Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of ethanol, C₂H₅OH(l), for the reaction 2C(s, graphite) + 3H₂(g) + ½O₂(g) → C₂H₅OH(l).

$\Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{C}, s) = -393.5$ kJ mol⁻¹
$\Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{H}_2, g) = -285.8$ kJ mol⁻¹
$\Delta H_c^\ominus(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}, l) = -1367.3$ kJ mol⁻¹ [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — draw the combustion cycle.** The reactants (2C, 3H₂) and the product (C₂H₅OH) all combust downwards to the same species, CO₂(g) and H₂O(l). Following the route — reactants down, then up the reverse of the product's combustion — gives Σ$\Delta H_c$(reactants) − Σ$\Delta H_c$(products). Oxygen does not combust, so it contributes nothing.  [M1: correct method with stoichiometric coefficients]

**Step 2 — substitute, multiplying each value by its coefficient.**
$\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}) = [2\times(-393.5) + 3\times(-285.8)] - [1\times(-1367.3)]$  [M1: correct substitution]

**Step 3 — evaluate.**
$= [-787.0 - 857.4] - [-1367.3]$
$= -1644.4 + 1367.3$
$= -277.1$ kJ mol⁻¹.  [A1: answer with sign and units]

*Note the coefficients: 2 mol of carbon and 3 mol of hydrogen each scale the per-mole combustion value. Forgetting the ×2 and ×3 is a classic error that would give a badly wrong number.*

sections.content: (HL) Born–Haber cycles, lattice enthalpy and enthalpy of solution

For an ionic solid, some enthalpy changes cannot be measured directly at all — you cannot assemble a crystal from a jar of isolated gaseous ions. The lattice enthalpy (here, the enthalpy of forming one mole of the ionic solid from its gaseous ions, an exothermic quantity) is found instead from a Born–Haber cycle: an enthalpy cycle that decomposes the formation of the ionic compound into a chain of measurable steps — atomisation of the elements, ionisation energies to make cations, electron affinities to make anions, and the lattice-formation step. Applying Hess's law around the closed cycle lets you solve for whichever single step is unknown, exactly as in the SL cycles. The enthalpy of solution — the enthalpy change when one mole of solute dissolves to give a very dilute solution — is likewise found from a cycle linking the lattice enthalpy to the hydration enthalpies of the separated ions.

items: **HL only:** Born–Haber cycles and lattice enthalpy are assessed at Higher Level; SL students are not examined on them.
A Born–Haber cycle is just Hess's law applied to an ionic compound, with the sum around the closed cycle equal to zero.
Lattice enthalpy is found by rearranging the cycle for the one step that cannot be measured directly.
Enthalpy of solution = lattice enthalpy (bond-breaking, endothermic in the reverse convention) combined with the hydration enthalpies of the gaseous ions (exothermic).

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Not reversing the sign of a reversed step** — if you travel a known reaction backwards in a cycle, flip its sign. An exothermic forward step becomes endothermic in reverse.
**Getting the subtraction order wrong** — formation is Σproducts − Σreactants; combustion is Σreactants − Σproducts. They are mirror images; picking the wrong one negates your answer.

**Forgetting that $\Delta H_f^\ominus$ of an element is zero** — elements in their standard states (H₂, O₂, Na, C) contribute 0, but they must still appear in the working, not be silently dropped or given a made-up value.
**Not multiplying by the stoichiometric coefficients** — enthalpy values are per mole, so 3 mol H₂ contributes $3\times\Delta H$, not one lot. Skipping the multiplier is a frequent and costly slip.
**Mismatched states** — H₂O(l) and H₂O(g) have different enthalpies; using the wrong state, or letting reactant and product states differ from the data, breaks the cycle.
**Dropping the sign or units on the final answer** — an enthalpy change without its sign (and kJ mol⁻¹) is incomplete and loses the answer mark, even when the arithmetic is right.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

This is the showcase for a calculation topic. In Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working — a method mark (M) or an answer mark (A) — and error-carried-forward (ECF) means a wrong number early on does not cost you every mark that follows, provided the method is written down. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line, and notice that two of the three marks survive an arithmetic slip.

sections.question:
Calculate the standard enthalpy change, $\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus$, for the reaction 2H₂S(g) + 3O₂(g) → 2SO₂(g) + 2H₂O(l), using standard enthalpies of formation:
$\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{H}_2\text{S}, g) = -21$ kJ mol⁻¹
$\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{SO}_2, g) = -297$ kJ mol⁻¹
$\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -286$ kJ mol⁻¹ [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

O₂(g) is an element in its standard state, so $\Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{O}_2) = 0$.

Using $\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{reactants})$ and multiplying each value by its coefficient:

$\sum\text{products} = 2(-297) + 2(-286) = -594 - 572 = -1166$ kJ
$\sum\text{reactants} = 2(-21) + 3(0) = -42$ kJ

$\Delta H_{reaction}^\ominus = -1166 - (-42) = -1124$ kJ mol⁻¹.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks:**

- **M1 — correct method.** Awarded for using the formation relationship Σ$\Delta H_f$(products) − Σ$\Delta H_f$(reactants), with O₂ taken as zero. This is a METHOD mark: it is credited for the correct approach even if a number is later mis-copied.
- **M1 — correct substitution.** Awarded for substituting the data with the right stoichiometric coefficients: $[2(-297) + 2(-286)] - [2(-21) + 3(0)]$. The ×2 and ×3 multipliers must be present. ECF applies — a candidate who mislabels one datum but substitutes it correctly into the right structure still earns this mark.
- **A1 — final answer with sign.** Awarded for $-1124$ kJ mol⁻¹, with the negative sign and units. Under ECF, if an earlier arithmetic slip fed through the correct method, the answer mark is still given on the candidate's own consistent figures.

**'Accept answers that round correctly.'** The engine accepts a final value that rounds correctly (e.g. $-1124$ or a legitimately rounded $-1120$ kJ mol⁻¹) and does not demand a fixed number of decimal places in the intermediate lines — it credits the correct method and a correctly-rounded, correctly-signed final answer.

**Bottom line:** of the 3 marks, 2 are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip and the answer mark is protected by ECF. A candidate who writes only '−1124' with no working risks losing 2 marks if the final number is wrong; one who shows the formula, the substitution and the coefficients keeps the method marks regardless.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with ECF. So always show the method: state the formula, substitute with the stoichiometric coefficients, include the zero for any element, and finish with a signed answer and units. The method marks and error-carried-forward are what protect your score when the final number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

Energy cycles are the reasoning engine behind much of what follows. Bond-enthalpy calculations are Hess's law with 'break all bonds' as the common intermediate; at HL, Born–Haber and solution cycles extend the same detour logic to ionic compounds; and the sign and size of $\Delta H$ feed directly into spontaneity and Gibbs energy later in R1. Master the habit — draw the cycle, follow the arrows, reverse the sign when you go backwards, multiply by the coefficients — and every enthalpy problem in the course becomes a variation on a method you already own.
